summary: O'Neil analyzes a scatter plot of nodes vs. edges in the dependency graphs of Johan de Jong's Stacks Project, finding a remarkably linear relationship with slope 1.86. She explores why mathematical proof structures would produce such clean, consistent data, considering how overlapping subgraphs and citation patterns might explain the ratio. Her most provocative conclusion is that the consistency reflects the internal cognitive patterns of the mathematician who created it—when things feel 'complicated enough,' he wraps up a result, and that threshold is remarkably consistent within one person's brain.
key_points: The scatter plot of graph complexity (nodes vs edges) for 10,445 mathematical results is unusually linear and tightly clustered around the best-fit line
Edges grow linearly rather than quadratically because only direct proof dependencies are shown, not all logical dependencies
The 1.86 slope can be partially explained by a toy model where results reference ~2 prior results, with the gap from 2.0 representing overlap between supporting concepts
The remarkable consistency likely reflects the mathematician's internal sense of when something is 'complicated enough' to become a distinct result
Different mathematicians would likely produce different slopes, and comparing to arXiv citation networks could be illuminating
money_quotes: As a data person, let me just say that data is never this clean.
There's something going on, some internal structure to these graphs that we should try to understand.
The only real explanation of the consistency above is my husband brain.
At some point, when he thinks things have gotten complicated enough, he says to himself 'It's time to wrap this stuff up and call it a result!' and then he does so.
That moment, when he's decided things are getting complicated enough, is very consistent internally to his brain.
If someone else created the stacks project, I'd expect to see another kind of plot, possibly also very consistent, but possibly with a different slope.
key_insight: The structure of mathematical knowledge graphs may reveal more about how individual mathematicians think than about the inherent structure of mathematics itself.
